Disentangling Similarity and Relatedness in Topic Models
Hanlin Xiao1,2, Mauricio A Álvarez2, Rainer Breitling1,3
1Manchester Institute of Biotechnology, The University of Manchester, UK
2Department of Computer Science, The University of Manchester, UK
3Department of Chemistry, The University of Manchester, UK
hanlin.xiao@postgrad.manchester.ac.uk
Abstract
The recent advancement of large language
models has spurred a growing trend of inte-
grating pre-trained language model (PLM)
embeddings into topic models, fundamen-
tally reshaping how topics capture seman-
tic structure. Classical models such as
Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) derive
topics from word co-occurrence statistics,
whereas PLM-augmented models anchor
these statistics to pre-trained embedding
spaces, imposing a prior that also favours
clustering of semantically similar words.
This structural difference can be captured
by the psycholinguistic dimensions ofthe-
matic relatednessandtaxonomic similarity
of the topic words. To disentangle these di-
mensions in topic models, we construct a
large synthetic benchmark of word pairs us-
ing LLM-based annotation to train a neural
scoring function. We apply this scorer to
a comprehensive evaluation across multiple
corpora and topic model families, reveal-
ing that different model families capture dis-
tinct semantic structure in their topics. We
further demonstrate that similarity and re-
latedness scores successfully predict down-
stream task performance depending on task
requirements. This paper establishes simi-
larity and relatedness as essential axes for
topic model evaluation and provides a reli-
able pipeline for characterising these across
model families and corpora.1
1 Introduction
Topic models discover latent thematic structure
from a corpus of documents by topics, each rep-
1Code and data will be made publicly available upon ac-
ceptance.resented as a distribution over words that co-
here around a common theme. For instance,
{baseball,playground,audience,win} suggests a
topic of baseball games. Traditional probabilis-
tic topic models, such as Latent Dirichlet Alloca-
tion (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003), rely on word co-
occurrence statistics to represent each topic as a
multinomial distribution over words and each doc-
ument as a mixture of topics. More recently, the
advent of contextual encoders and representation
learning methods (Devlin et al., 2019; Pennington
et al., 2014) has led to a new paradigm of PLM-
augmented topic models (Wu et al., 2024b; Groo-
tendorst, 2022; Wu et al., 2023, 2024c; Reuter
et al., 2025). For example, TNTM (Reuter et al.,
2025) employs a variational autoencoder architec-
ture that models each document as a mixture of
topics, with each topic parameterized as a multi-
variate Gaussian in word embedding space, while
simpler approaches, such as BERT-KT (Sia et al.,
2020), directly cluster mean-pooled BERT em-
beddings and apply corpus-level term-frequency
weighting to extract topic candidates. PLM-
augmented topic models have demonstrated strong
performance on standard evaluation metrics, topic
diversity, coherence (Wu et al., 2024b), and also
embedding-based measures (Aletras and Steven-
son, 2013; Terragni et al., 2021b),
However, the previous evaluation metrics over-
look a fundamental difference between these two
families of models: probabilistic topic models
tend to capturethematic relatedness, associative
connections between contextually co-occurring
words (e.g.,coffee–mug), as they rely on co-
occurrence statistics; whereas PLM-augmented
topic models, by leveraging embedding proxim-
ity, additionally capturetaxonomic similarity, sub-arXiv:2603.10619v1  [cs.CL]  11 Mar 2026

stitutional closeness between categorically related
words (e.g.,coffee–tea). This distinction is well
established in the psycholinguistic and NLP com-
munities (Agirre et al., 2009; Hill et al., 2015; Mir-
man and Graziano, 2012; Landrigan and Mirman,
2016), and can be readily observed by inspect-
ing topic examples from models such as BERT-
KT (Sia et al., 2020) (Appendix A.3, Table 7);
however, no existing metric directly quantifies this
two-dimensional distinction. Without such a met-
ric, this distinction goes unrecognized in model
evaluation, further obscuring how model choice
translates to downstream task performance.
In this paper, we address this gap by training a
two-dimensional neural scorer for word pair sim-
ilarity and relatedness using a large-scale LLM-
annotated synthetic dataset. Using this scorer, we
conduct a comprehensive survey of topic model
behavior across multiple corpora, revealing a con-
sistent pattern: co-occurrence-based models in-
cline toward relatedness, while PLM-augmented
models incline toward similarity. This distinc-
tion proves to be not merely descriptive but pre-
dictive: in downstream task experiments, tasks
whose nature favors relatedness are better served
by relatedness-inclined models, and vice versa.
Beyond these core results, we employ the pro-
posed metric to further quantitatively analyze top-
ics and corpora.
The main contributions of this paper include:
• We introduce a novel two-dimensional eval-
uation framework that explicitly disentangles
taxonomic similarity from thematic related-
ness in topic models, addressing a fundamen-
tal gap in how the semantic structure of topics
is evaluated.
• We construct a large-scale synthetic bench-
mark of approximately 51K word pairs sam-
pled from existing knowledge networks with
LLM-imputed annotations, and train a neural
scorer that reliably quantifies both semantic
dimensions.
• We conduct a comprehensive evaluation of
topic model families across multiple corpora,
revealing that the similarity–relatedness dis-
tinction is not merely descriptive but predic-
tive of downstream task performance, val-
idating the explanatory power of disentan-
gling these two semantic axes.2 Related Work
2.1 Topic Models and Evaluation
Topic modeling has evolved through several gen-
erations: probabilistic generative models such as
LDA (Blei et al., 2003) and its extensions (e.g.,
HDP (Teh et al., 2006), CTM (Lafferty and Blei,
2005)); neural variational approaches (Srivastava
and Sutton, 2017; Miao et al., 2016), which re-
place Dirichlet sampling with V AE-based infer-
ence; and PLM-augmented topic models (Groo-
tendorst, 2022; Bianchi et al., 2021; Reuter
et al., 2025), which anchor the topics to pre-
trained language model embedding spaces such as
BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and GloVe (Penning-
ton et al., 2014). Standard evaluation relies on
coherence metrics (UCI, UMass, NPMI) (Mimno
et al., 2011; Röder et al., 2015) that assess word
co-occurrence patterns, diversity metrics (e.g.,
topic diversity and topic uniqueness) (Terragni
et al., 2021a; Wu et al., 2024d) and embedding-
based methods that measure the quality in the
embedding space (Aletras and Stevenson, 2013;
Rahimi et al., 2024). Prior work has also noted
that coherence scores do not reliably correlate with
human judgment (Chang et al., 2009; Lau et al.,
2014). While topic models have been evaluated
on downstream tasks such as document classifi-
cation and information retrieval (McAuliffe and
Blei, 2007; Boyd-Graber et al., 2017), these evalu-
ations have not connected performance differences
to the semantic structure of the topics themselves.
2.2 Similarity vs. Relatedness
The distinction between taxonomic similarity and
thematic relatedness is established in natural lan-
guage processing and psycholinguistics commu-
nities (Mirman and Graziano, 2012; Budanitsky
and Hirst, 2006). However, existing benchmarks
tend to focus on a single dimension or con-
flate both. Similarity-oriented datasets include
SimLex-999 (Hill et al., 2015) and RG-65 (Ruben-
stein and Goodenough, 1965), while related-
ness/association benchmarks include WordSim-
353 (Finkelstein et al., 2002) and MEN (Bruni
et al., 2014). Although TxThmNorms (Landrigan
and Mirman, 2016) and Agirre et al. (2009) pro-
vide both taxonomic and thematic signals, they re-
main small and noisy.
There are methods predicting each dimension
separately using distributional methods (Turney
and Pantel, 2010; Pennington et al., 2014; Bo-

janowski et al., 2017), contextual transformer em-
beddings (e.g., BERT-based representations) (De-
vlin et al., 2019; Gupta and Jaggi, 2021;
Gao et al., 2021), and knowledge-based mea-
sures (e.g., WordNet and Wikipedia-based re-
latedness) (Budanitsky and Hirst, 2006; Resnik,
1995; Gabrilovich and Markovitch, 2007), but
none jointly predict both on a continuous two-
dimensional scale.
3 Method
3.1 Similarity–Relatedness Scorer
3.1.1 Training Data Construction
Building a robust similarity–relatedness neural
scorer requires high-quality training data that dis-
tinguishes these two overlapping yet distinct se-
mantic dimensions. Historically, various word-
pair datasets have been curated to evaluate similar-
ity and relatedness (Rubenstein and Goodenough,
1965; Finkelstein et al., 2002; Agirre et al., 2009;
Bruni et al., 2014; Hill et al., 2015). However, ex-
isting benchmarks typically focus on a single di-
mension and suffer from systematic conflation of
the two (Table 6, Appendix A.1). Combining all
existing manually annotated datasets yields only
approximately 9,000 word pairs of noisy data,
which is insufficient to fully exploit the capacity
of a neural scoring model.
To address these limitations, we sampled from
existing knowledge networks and employed an
LLM Imputationapproach to curate large-scale
training data. By targeting specific word rela-
tions, including shared synsets and co-hyponyms
in WordNet (Miller, 1995), associative edges (Re-
latedTo,UsedFor,PartOf,AtLocation,Causes) in
ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017), cosine-nearest
neighbors in the BERT embedding space (Devlin
et al., 2019), and controlled negatives from cross-
domain and cross-POS pairings, we obtained can-
didate word pairs whose relationships span the full
spectrum of similarity and relatedness. These can-
didate pairs were then annotated using DeepSeek-
V3 (Liu et al., 2024). The prompt provides explicit
scoring anchors, low-relatedness calibration ex-
amples, and critical distinction rules that delineate
synonyms, complementary pairs, same-category
pairs, and antonyms across both dimensions (see
Appendix B.3 for the full curation details).
Through this pipeline, we curated a dataset
of 51,523 word pairs that offers balanced scor-
ing across the similarity–relatedness spectrum andbroad lexical diversity through its multi-source de-
sign. The dataset could be expanded to potentially
improve the scorer further, as our scaling-law anal-
ysis shows that performance continues to improve
at larger scales (Figure 6, Appendix B.4); how-
ever, the current size already achieves a strong per-
formance baseline.
3.1.2 Neural Network Architecture and
Training
We train a neural network-based scoring function
that takes a word pair as input and jointly pre-
dicts similarity and relatedness scores using the
synthetic dataset.
For input representation, we use GloVe
840B 300-dimensional embeddings (Pennington
et al., 2014). From each embedding pair
(e1,e2), we compute four interaction features
[e1+e2,e1−e2,e1⊙e2,|e1−e2|](Mou et al.,
2016; Conneau et al., 2017). These features
are concatenated with an 8-dimensional WordNet-
derived feature vector and passed through a mul-
tilayer perceptron that produces a 2-D output rep-
resenting the predicted similarity and relatedness
scores. To encourage the model to learn a mean-
ingful separation between the two dimensions, we
apply a small regularization term penalizing the
squared gap between the output scores. Full ar-
chitectural details and hyperparameter settings are
reported in Appendix B.4.
3.2 Topic Model Atlas
3.2.1 Topic Model Taxonomy
Having obtained the trained scorer, we apply it
to a comprehensive evaluation of topic models
across multiple corpora. In total, 13 models are
evaluated on six corpora. These models span
several methodological families: traditional prob-
abilistic models, neural V AE-based extensions,
embedding-clustering approaches based on PLMs,
and embedding-augmented V AE topic models. A
complete inventory of all models is provided in Ta-
ble 11 (Appendix B.1).
3.2.2 Corpora
The six corpora span a range of domains,
document lengths, and collection sizes:
Reuters (Lewis, 1997), M10 (Lim and Bun-
tine, 2015), DBLP (Ley, 2002), ACL (Bird et al.,
2008), BBC (Greene and Cunningham, 2006),
and 20NewsGroups (Lang, 1995). These include
domain-specific scientific collections as well

as general news and forum discussion corpora
covering both long and short documents and
varying corpus sizes. Detailed corpus statis-
tics and preprocessing settings are provided in
Appendix B.6.
3.2.3 Atlas Construction and Consistency
Analysis
For each (model, corpus) pair, we run the topic
model to generatek= 20 topics and retain the top
10 words from each run. To obtain robust esti-
mates, each model is run 10 times on each corpus
with different random seeds, yielding a substantial
number of independent topic sets across the atlas.
For each topic produced in a given run, we com-
pute the pairwise similarity and relatedness scores
over the top five words using the trained scorer
and average these to obtain a single 2D metric per
topic. This procedure results in, for each model,
up to 60 scored topic sets (six corpora times ten
runs), providing sufficient data for reliable statis-
tical analysis. To summarize a model’s position
along the similarity–relatedness spectrum, we de-
fine theshifted normalized gapas follows: sim-
ilarity and relatedness scores are first normalized
to[0,1]within each corpus, and the gap (relat-
edness minus similarity) is then shifted so that
the random-word baseline sits at zero. Positive
values indicate a relatedness-leaning profile rela-
tive to random, while negative values indicate a
similarity-leaning profile.
To verify that each model’s similarity–
relatedness profile is consistent across corpora,
we compute Kendall’s coefficient of concordance
W(Kendall, 1948) over the model rankings from
all six corpora.
However, not all entries in the full model–
corpus matrix could be obtained in practice. Sev-
eral (model, corpus) pairs suffered from topic col-
lapse or prohibitive running times, necessitating
exclusion. A full list is provided in Table 12 (Ap-
pendix B.2). Despite these exclusions, a total of
886 (model, corpus) runs were successfully com-
pleted, providing a sufficiently comprehensive ba-
sis for the subsequent analysis.
3.3 Downstream Tasks
The atlas analysis assigns each topic model a po-
sition in the similarity–relatedness space. We ex-
pect this metric to serve as a predictor of down-
stream performance on tasks with different se-
mantic requirements. To test this hypothesis, weevaluate on three downstream tasks, two favor-
ing relatedness-inclined models and one favoring
similarity-inclined models; the reasoning behind
each task’s expected alignment is detailed below.
Not all atlas models appear in every task due to im-
plementation constraints; model coverage is noted
per task.
3.3.1 Task A: Event Monitoring (Reuters)
The first task evaluates topic models on event
monitoring using the Reuters corpus (Lewis,
1997), a multi-label collection of 90 thematic cat-
egories where each document may be assigned
to one or more of them. For each category in
the training set, we embed all associated docu-
ments as topic vectors and compute a centroid to
represent that category. At test time, each doc-
ument is similarly embedded and ranked against
all category centroids. Performance is measured
by Hit@3 (whether at least one of the top-3 pre-
dicted categories matches a true label) and Re-
call@3, the fraction of true labels recovered by
the top-3 predictions. Since event monitoring re-
lies on recognizing which words co-occur to form
coherent themes, it favors models that capture this
co-occurrence structure; such models tend to pro-
duce topics with higher relatedness, a signal di-
rectly quantified by our metric.
3.3.2 Task B: Category Retrieval (Reuters)
The second task also uses the Reuters corpus but
evaluates category retrieval via nearest-neighbor
consistency. Each document is embedded as a
topic vector, and for every document, we retrieve
its 10 nearest neighbors in topic space, checking
whether their category labels match the query doc-
ument’s label. Performance is measured by the
same-category rate (SCR), the fraction of the 10
nearest neighbors that share at least one category
label with the query document. By the same rea-
soning as Task A, we expect relatedness-inclined
models to perform better.
3.3.3 Task C: Synonym Pair Retrieval
Consistency
The third task is designed to favor similarity-
inclined models. We manually curate 50 strict syn-
onym pairs (e.g.,increase/rise,decline/fall,com-
pany/firm); and, for each word in a pair, com-
pute the conditional probabilityP(w|d)of word
wgiven documentd, marginalized over topics us-
ing the model’s native topic–word distribution. We

then select the top-10 highest-scoring documents
for each word and measure the overlap between
the two ranked lists using Jaccard similarity. The
underlying intuition is that synonyms, being se-
mantically interchangeable, should induce similar
document rankings under a model that captures se-
mantic similarity.
Because this task does not require ground-truth
labels, we evaluate it across all six corpora, using
cross-corpus consistency as evidence of validity.
Since the task requires access to the native topic–
word distribution, which is difficult to extract uni-
formly across all architectures, we include only a
representative subset of models spanning the ma-
jor model families; per-corpus performance and
model coverage are reported in Appendix A.4.
3.3.4 Regression Analysis
To test whether our similarity–relatedness metric
predicts downstream performance, we conduct a
series of single-factor linear regressions for each
task, regressing task performance on each predic-
tor individually: the model’s similarity score, re-
latedness score, the gap between them, as well
asC Vcoherence (Röder et al., 2015) and topic
diversity—two widely adopted intrinsic metrics
for evaluating topic model quality. For Task C,
which is evaluated across multiple corpora, we
introduce a corpus-level intercept to account for
baseline variation while fitting a single shared
slope across all corpora. We report statistical sig-
nificance for each predictor.
4 Results
In this section, we focus on reporting general find-
ings, including cross-corpus consistency of model
behavior and regression analyses linking the sim-
ilarity and relatedness scores to downstream task
performance, together with targeted discussion of
notable patterns. Detailed per-model comparisons
and full task results are provided in the Appendix.
4.1 Scorer Validation
We evaluate the predictive accuracy of the pro-
posed neural scorer for both similarity and related-
ness through a two-fold testing approach. First, we
assess the model on a held-out set during training,
with the learning trajectory across epochs depicted
in Figure 5 (Appendix B.4). Although the training
and test loss curves visibly diverge, the training
loss decreases steadily, confirming successful op-
timization. This gap is expected, as predicting ex-Table 1: Spearman rank correlation (ρ s) on 651 Tx-

act scores for unseen word pairs is inherently diffi-
cult. Importantly, the Spearman correlation on the
test set starts high and improves gradually before
stabilizing. This shows that even if the absolute
predictions exhibit a gap, the model successfully
learns the underlying semantic structure and accu-
rately ranks unseen data without overfitting.
Moreover, we evaluate the model’s generaliza-
tion capabilities on an external benchmark, Tx-
ThmNorms (Landrigan and Mirman, 2016), which
provides human-annotated scores for both seman-
tic dimensions. As detailed in Table 1, we com-
pare the neural scorer against BERT cosine simi-
larity and established WordNet features. The re-
sults show that the neural scorer not only achieves
similarity predictions competitive with the struc-
turally informed WordNet baselines, while sub-
stantially outperforming BERT, but also demon-
strates a distinct proficiency in predicting related-
ness. Moreover, the model successfully disentan-
gles the two semantic dimensions, as evidenced by
its ability to accurately predict the directional gap
between relatedness and similarity scores. This
accurate prediction and appropriate differentiation
between similarity and relatedness validate the
neural model’s capacity to operate within the two-
dimensional semantic space. Table 2 presents ex-
amples of the model’s scoring.
4.1.1 Robustness and Generalizability
To optimize the configuration of the neural scorer,
we conducted a comprehensive ablation study
evaluating key component choices: the static word
embedding space, the input representation, the in-
clusion of WordNet features, and the network ar-
chitecture. Because the scorer is designed to eval-
uate topic words generated from external corpora,
its ability to generalize to entirely unseen vocab-
ulary is critical. To explicitly test the general-
izability, the ablation study evaluated configura-
tions across three distinct generalization scenarios:
a pair-held-out test set, a word-held-out test set,
and an external evaluation using the TxThmNorms

Beyond optimizing the architecture, we con-

between the synthetic training dataset and the
downstream evaluation corpora (Figure 4, Ap-

generally broad enough to cover a substantial por-
tion of the target vocabularies. This high overlap
naturally lowers the generalization burden on the
model. However, highly domain-specific corpora,
such as ACL, exhibit distinct lexical regions that
are uncovered by the synthetic data. This varying
degree of coverage directly influences the model’s
downstream evaluation performance, as will be
discussed in Section 5.1.
Furthermore, because the extracted WordNet
features are inherently biased toward nouns, we
specifically tested whether the model could effec-
tively process other parts of speech. As shown in
Table 15 (Appendix B.4.2), the model performs
robustly across POS divisions. Finally, since the
scorer relies on static embeddings, we verified that
it does not collapse polysemous words to a sin-

4.2 Topic Model Atlas
Figure 1 presents a comprehensive comparison
of topic model relatedness and similarity on the
Reuters corpus. Models are ordered by gap rank,
with PLM-augmented models placed on the right-
hand side. From the gap scores, it is clear that
most PLM-augmented models incline toward sim-
ilarity, with a few exceptions which are discussed
in Section 5. Their rankings are also largely pre-
served across different corpora, as shown in Ap-
pendix A.2.
To formally assess this cross-corpus consis-
tency, we calculated Kendall’s coefficient of con-
cordanceW(Kendall, 1948) for all models across
the six evaluation corpora. As summarized in
Table 3, all three axes yield strong concordance
(W= 0.58–0.64). Given the substantial differ-
ences in domain, vocabulary, and document length
across the six corpora, this level of agreement indi-
cates that a model’s similarity–relatedness profile

4.3 Downstream Task Prediction
Table 5 presents selected model performance and
rankings across all three tasks (full per-model re-
sults are provided in Appendix A.4). As antic-
ipated, Tasks A and B, which favor relatedness,
generally yield stronger performance with tradi-
tional co-occurrence-based and neural V AE-based
models, whereas the PLM-augmented models ex-
cel on the similarity-favoring Task C, and vice
versa. Single-factor regression results assessing
the predictive power of relatedness and similarity
are reported in Table 4. Relatedness emerges as a
stable predictor of performance on Tasks A and B,
while similarity and gap statistics consistently pre-Table 5: Performance rankings for selected models

that the two scores capture intrinsically distinct se-
mantic properties of topic models, each selectively

low explanatory power and occasional sign rever-

5 Further Analysis
5.1 Corpus Properties as Moderators
Across the atlas, we observe that document
length, domain specificity, and vocabulary char-
acteristics (Table 17) systematically influence
how models score on similarity and relatedness.
Topics derived from the ACL corpus consis-
tently exhibit elevated similarity yet compara-
tively low relatedness. The high similarity re-
flects a narrow technical vocabulary in which
many core terms are near-synonymous (e.g.,lex-
ical/semantic,speech/language), while the low
relatedness arises from the corpus’s domain-
specific vocabulary tail: topics frequently contain
acronyms, proper names, and fragment-like tokens
that compress relatedness scores.
Moreover, 20 Newsgroups yields the least
consistent cross-model rankings, driven by two
vocabulary-induced forces that pull similarity and
relatedness in opposite directions. First, because
it consists of informal discussions, 20 News-
groups contains frequent unnormalized contrac-
tions and discourse tokens (e.g.,dontin 21.3%
of documents anddoesntin 7.7%) that are es-
sentially absent from the other corpora. These
tokens form tight style clusters that our scorer
can assign high similarity yet low relatedness
(e.g.,dont/doesnt/isnt/wouldnt/wasnt), and co-
occurrence models surface them far more of-
ten than embedding-based models: contraction-
bearing topics comprise 26% of LDA topics but

only 5% of BERT-KT topics on 20 Newsgroups.
Second, outside this conversational layer, 20
Newsgroups topics are dominated by plain, every-
day vocabulary that lacks the intricate, specialised
expressions found in edited-text corpora; unlike
ACL, where morphological families and near-
synonyms abound (e.g.,parse/parsing/parser), 20
Newsgroups domains are discussed with common
words that are thematically associated but rarely
substitutable, so similarity stays depressed while
PLM-augmented models push relatedness sharply
upward. These competing forces make similarity,
relatedness, and gap rankings on 20 Newsgroups
markedly less stable than on the edited-text cor-
pora. This further suggests that for noisy, informal
corpora of this kind, PLM-augmented models may
be preferable, as they inject stronger semantic pri-
ors that yield more meaningful topic clusters—a
consideration particularly relevant for the design
of V AE-based models.
5.2 BERTopic: Document-Level Embeddings
Despite being a PLM-augmented model,
BERTopic consistently achieves relatedness
scores comparable to co-occurrence-based models
while exhibiting only moderate similarity, and
performs strongly on the relatedness-favoring
Tasks A and B, well above other PLM-augmented
models. This is because BERTopic employs
Sentence-BERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019)
embeddings primarily forsentence-level doc-
ument clustering, not direct word-level topic
construction. Topic words are then extracted via
c-TF-IDF, which ranks terms by their frequency
within a cluster relative to their frequency across
all clusters. This criterion rewards terms that
recur consistently throughout a cluster and are
distinctive to it, but does not explicitly encourage
near-synonymy among top-ranked words. Syn-
onyms tend to substitute for one another rather
than co-occur, splitting counts across alternative
surface forms and thereby reducing each form’s
individual TF score. As a result, BERTopic
topics remain thematically coherent yet not
synonym-rich, consistent with its high relatedness
but moderate similarity profile.
5.3 BERT-KT’s Inflated Similarity and
ECRTM’s Low Relatedness
On theReuterscorpus, BERT-KT and ECRTM
yield divergent metric anomalies: the former ex-
hibits spuriously high similarity scores, whilethe latter demonstrates exceptionally low related-
ness. Investigating these outliers reveals contrast-
ing failure modes regarding how each model uti-
lizes the embedding space. BERT-KT’s inflated
similarity originates fromgeneric-topic artifacts.
The model tends to cluster high-frequency regis-
ter words, such as reporting verbs (told,said) and
temporal markers, into topics that appear coher-
ent in embedding space but remain semantically
vacuous. On average, 28% of the topics gener-
ated per run consist almost entirely of such lexical
terms, which receive high scores from our neu-
ral scorer in terms of similarity. This artificially
inflates BERT-KT’s overall performance despite
the lack of thematic substance, highlighting a no-
table caveat when relying solely on embedding-
based similarity. Conversely, ECRTM’s deflated
relatedness stems fromrare-token clustering. The
model’s embedding clustering regularizer, which
enforces topic separation via optimal transport,
inadvertently biases the model toward geomet-
ric outliers. To maximize inter-topic dispersion,
ECRTM groups rare, isolated entities or sur-
names (e.g.,baird,brusca,crosby) that possess
distinct embeddings but lack stable document-
level co-occurrence. Consequently, approximately
63% of ECRTM’s top-word pairs exhibit zero co-
occurrence onReuters, compared to only 4% for
LDA and 7% for BERTopic. This validates the di-
agnostic utility of the relatedness dimension, as it
successfully exposes genuine co-occurrence fail-
ures that embedding-based metrics alone would
overlook.
5.4 Divergence Between Coherence and
Relatedness
Coherence and relatedness both quantify asso-
ciations between topic words, yet they opera-
tionalize distinct forms of association. Coher-
ence measures corpus-conditional co-occurrence,
rewarding word pairs that frequently appear to-
gether within the training corpus. Relatedness,
in contrast, reflects a semantic prior induced by
general-language supervision, independent of the
target corpus. The two metrics may therefore di-
verge in specialized domains. For instance, in
ECRTM on ACL, a topic containing the words
patr, ewan, calder, unification, gazdarattains
high coherence because these terms are densely
coupled within a specific theoretical and citation
community centered on unification grammar and

the PATR framework (Gazdar et al., 1985). Yet
the same topic receives a low relatedness score,
since a scorer trained on broad semantic relations
does not encode such domain-specific author- and
framework-level associations. This gap serves as
a quantitative signal of corpus specificity: co-
herence captures internal structural regularities,
while relatedness captures domain-general lexical
semantics.
6 Discussion
6.1 Limitations
Vocabulary GeneralizabilityThe synthetic
dataset is constructed from samples drawn from
ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017), WordNet (Miller,
1995), and the BERT vocabulary (Devlin et al.,
2019), while the static embeddings rely on
GloVe’s (Pennington et al., 2014) capacity to
generalize to unseen word pairs. Although
these resources encode broad common-sense
and lexical knowledge, they tend to overestimate
associations among high-frequency, generic
words and perform poorly on domain-specific ter-
minology. For instance, highly specialized terms
commonly encountered in venues such as ACL
may not be adequately captured. Consequently,
the neural scorer should be interpreted alongside
complementary metrics, such as topic coherence,
which can capture corpus-internal regularities
that the neural approach alone may overlook.
Moreover, obtaining domain-specific training data
or fine-tuning the underlying embeddings on the
target domain could substantially improve the
scorer’s coverage of specialised vocabulary.
Human AnnotationOur evaluation relies
mostly on automated metrics and LLM-based im-
putation. Human annotation of topic quality and
curated evaluation datasets spanning diverse spe-
cialist domains remains scarce. Direct human
judgments would provide a valuable check on the
patterns identified here and help establish more re-
liable benchmarks for interpreting metric behav-
ior.
Multilingual SupportOur findings are con-
strained by the English-centric nature of both
the evaluation resources and the corpora exam-
ined. Extending this framework to multilingual
settings would require comparable semantic re-
sources across languages, which remain unevenly
developed. However, recent advances in multilin-
gual language models and text encoders (Conneauet al., 2020; Wang et al., 2024) offer a promising
foundation for such extensions in future work.
Model CoverageThe static embeddings and
topic model architectures examined here repre-
sent only a subset of available approaches. In-
corporating more recent embedding methods and
topic models equipped with more capable en-
coders (Warner et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2024)
would broaden the scope of the evaluation and
may reveal additional performance patterns not
captured by the current framework.
6.2 Applications
The proposed two-dimensional metric offers a
diagnostic capability absent from conventional
measures. By inspecting a model’s similarity–
relatedness profile, practitioners can explicitly
characterize the type of semantic structure being
captured. For example, in V AE-based topic mod-
els augmented with pretrained embeddings, devel-
opers can track how increasing the weight of the
embedding component shifts the balance toward
taxonomic similarity, enabling principled archi-
tectural and hyperparameter decisions. The met-
ric also complements existing benchmarking axes
by exposing structural trade-offs that coherence
or diversity alone cannot reveal. Moreover, the
predictive relationship between the 2D metric and
downstream performance suggests that the metric
can guide practitioners in matching models to task
requirements. For instance, retrieval-augmented
generation pipelines (Lewis et al., 2020; Tran and
Litman, 2024) could leverage relatedness-inclined
models for broad recall and similarity-inclined
models for precise reranking.
When applied to a new corpus, the metric pro-
file across multiple models could serve as a diag-
nostic of corpus-level semantic structure, reveal-
ing the specificity of its vocabulary. Such sig-
nals can inform preprocessing choices, including
domain-specific stopword filtering or vocabulary
normalization prior to topic modeling. Beyond
topic model evaluation, the neural scorer func-
tions as a general-purpose instrument for quantify-
ing taxonomic similarity and thematic relatedness
over arbitrary word sets, supporting tasks such as
evaluating keyword extraction outputs (Wu et al.,
2024a) or assessing the semantic cohesion of word
clusters.

7 Conclusion
This work introduces a two-dimensional evalua-
tion framework that disentanglestaxonomic sim-
ilarityfromthematic relatednessin topic model
outputs. By training a neural scorer on a large-
scale, synthetic LLM-annotated dataset, we quan-
tify both dimensions across a comprehensive at-
las of topic models and corpora, revealing that the
similarity–relatedness profile is a stable, intrinsic
property of each model family: co-occurrence-
based models consistently incline toward related-
ness, while PLM-augmented models incline to-
ward similarity. Moreover, this distinction proves
predictive of downstream performance, while es-
tablished metrics such as coherence and diversity
cannot replicate. These findings enhance topic
model evaluation toward understanding what kind
of semantic structure a model captures, opening
avenues for task-aware model selection and prin-
cipled tuning of embedding-augmented architec-
tures.

A.5 Polysemy Sense Preservation
Method.We select 50 polysemous words with≥3 WordNet noun synsets spanning≥2 coarse semantic
domains. For each word, we group synsets by domain and collect taxonomic neighbors (synonyms,
hypernyms, meronyms), yielding 194 sense clusters for the similarity head. For the relatedness head,
we build thematic clusters via ConceptNet associative relations. A cluster ispreservedif its mean score
exceeds the random-word baseline by Cohen’sd >2.0;weakif1.0< d≤2.0; andlostotherwise.

B.3.2 LLM Annotation
All 51,523 pairs were annotated using DeepSeek-V3 (Liu et al., 2024) via the prompt in Section B.3.3,
with temperature 0.3 and a single scoring pass per pair (n=1). A pilot study scoring the first∼10K pairs
three times showed high inter-sample stability, justifying single-pass annotation.
B.3.3 LLM Scoring Prompt
Each word pair is scored by prompting DeepSeek-V3 with the template below. The placeholders
{word1}and{word2}are filled per pair. The system message is:“You are an expert linguist neural
specializing in semantic similarity and relatedness. You provide precise numerical scores with brief,
clear reasoning. ”The model outputsSIM:,REL:, andREASON:fields; only the numeric scores are
retained. The full user prompt follows.
# Understanding Semantic Similarity vs. Semantic Relatedness
**Semantic Similarity **(taxonomic/categorical overlap):
- Measures if words refer to the SAME TYPE of thing or share properties
- Focus: "Could these words substitute for each other in some contexts?"
- Key: Shared category membership, overlapping features
Examples:
*stair/staircase: 0.95 (essentially the same thing, just different forms)

*car/automobile: 0.98 (perfect synonyms)
*cat/dog: 0.85 (both pets, mammals, similar roles)
*cup/mug: 0.90 (both drinking vessels, very similar)
*doctor/hospital: 0.10 (person vs. place - different categories)
*monkey/banana: 0.05 (animal vs. fruit - different categories)
**Semantic Relatedness **(thematic/associative connection in DIFFERENT roles):
- Measures if words appear together in real-world scenarios playing DIFFERENT roles
- Focus: "Do these words combine in contexts, where each plays a distinct role?"
- Key: Functional association, complementary relationship, NOT substitution
Examples (Nouns):
*doctor/hospital: 0.95 (doctors work IN hospitals - strong functional link)
*monkey/banana: 0.90 (monkeys eat bananas - strong thematic association)
*key/lock: 0.95 (keys open locks - complementary function)
*dog/leash: 0.90 (dogs are walked on leashes)
*stair/staircase: 0.15 (these are the same thing, they don’t co-occur)
*car/automobile: 0.10 (perfect synonyms don’t appear together)
*cat/dog: 0.55 (both pets, but less direct functional connection)
*cup/mug: 0.50 (similar items, but don’t strongly appear together)
Examples (Adjectives - co-occurring traits):
*pretty/popular: 0.85 (traits that often describe the same person together)
*wealthy/successful: 0.80 (associated outcomes, often co-describe people)
*young/energetic: 0.75 (associated characteristics)
*sunny/happy: 0.80 (mood/weather association, "sunny disposition")
*old/wise: 0.70 (cultural association, traits that go together)
*big/large: 0.10 (synonyms - they substitute, not co-occur)
*happy/joyful: 0.15 (synonyms - they substitute, not co-occur)
*hot/cold: 0.35 (antonyms - contrasted, don’t co-describe same thing)
Examples (Verbs - sequential/complementary actions):
*cook/eat: 0.85 (sequential actions - you cook then eat)
*write/read: 0.80 (complementary actions - one writes, another reads)
*teach/learn: 0.85 (complementary roles in same activity)
*buy/sell: 0.80 (complementary transaction roles)
*plant/harvest: 0.75 (sequential farming actions)
*run/sprint: 0.10 (synonyms - they substitute, not co-occur)
*speak/talk: 0.15 (synonyms - they substitute, not co-occur)
*begin/start: 0.10 (synonyms - they substitute, not co-occur)
**Critical Distinctions: **
1.**Synonyms/Variants **: HIGH similarity (0.9+), LOW relatedness (0.1-0.3)
- They mean the same thing, so they REPLACE each other, not co-occur
- Nouns: car/automobile, couch/sofa, stair/staircase
- Adjectives: big/large, happy/joyful, smart/intelligent
- Verbs: run/sprint, speak/talk, begin/start
2.**Complementary Pairs **: LOW similarity (0.0-0.3), HIGH relatedness (0.8+)
- Different types of things that appear together
- Nouns: doctor/hospital, pen/paper, bread/butter
- Adjectives: pretty/popular, wealthy/successful (co-occurring traits)
- Verbs: cook/eat, teach/learn, buy/sell (sequential/complementary actions)
3.**Same Category **: HIGH similarity (0.7+), MODERATE relatedness (0.4-0.7)
- Same type but don’t necessarily appear together
- Nouns: cat/dog, apple/orange, hammer/screwdriver
- Verbs: walk/run, eat/drink, read/write (similar actions, not necessarily sequential)
4.**Antonyms **: LOW similarity (0.0-0.2), LOW-MODERATE relatedness (0.3-0.6)
- Opposite properties but contextually contrasted
- Adjectives: hot/cold, happy/sad, old/new
- Verbs: come/go, rise/fall, push/pull
## Similarity Scoring Scale
0.95-1.00: Perfect synonyms or very close variants (car/automobile, stair/staircase)
0.85-0.95: Same specific category (cat/dog, cup/mug, knife/fork)
0.70-0.85: Same broad category (apple/orange, car/truck, shirt/pants)
0.50-0.70: Related categories with overlap (fruit/vegetable, bird/insect)
0.30-0.50: Loose categorical connection (animal/plant, vehicle/building)
0.10-0.30: Different categories, minimal overlap (person/place, object/emotion)
0.00-0.10: Completely different categories (car/happiness, doctor/banana)
**special cases: **
- Antonyms (hot/cold): 0.0-0.2 (opposite properties)
- Near-synonyms: 0.9+
## Relatedness Scoring Scale
0.90-1.00: Extremely strong functional/thematic link (doctor/hospital, key/lock, bread/butter)
0.75-0.90: Strong association, appear together often (teacher/school, rain/umbrella, pretty/popular, cook/eat, teach/learn)
0.60-0.75: Clear contextual connection (book/library, dog/park, young/energetic, plant/harvest)
0.40-0.60: Moderate association, same domain (cat/dog, apple/orange, walk/run, eat/drink)
0.25-0.40: Weak but present connection (antonyms like hot/cold, come/go, rise/fall)
0.10-0.25: Minimal association (car/tree, happy/blue)
0.00-0.10: No meaningful association OR synonyms that substitute each other (car/automobile, big/large, run/sprint)
**special cases: **
- Perfect synonyms/variants (car/automobile, big/large, run/sprint, speak/talk): 0.0-0.2 (they substitute, not co-occur)
- Antonyms (hot/cold, happy/sad, come/go): 0.3-0.5 (contrasted but not as strong as complementary pairs)
- Complementary pairs (doctor/hospital, key/lock): 0.9+ (strong functional association)
- Co-occurring adjective traits (pretty/popular, wealthy/successful): 0.75-0.90 (traits that describe same entity together)
- Sequential/complementary verbs (cook/eat, teach/learn, buy/sell): 0.75-0.90 (actions that go together in sequence or roles)
# Task
Rate BOTH similarity and relatedness between:
WORD1: "{word1}"
WORD2: "{word2}"
# Rules
- Use the most common contemporary English sense for each word.
- If multiple senses are similarly common, choose the sense that makes the pair most interpretable in general usage; do not choose a rare technical sense.
- Treat simple inflectional variants (plural/singular, tense) as the same lemma for similarity.
- Do NOT write intermediate reasoning. Output only the required lines.
- Use exactly 2 decimal places.
# Output format (exactly)
SIM: <0.00-1.00>
REL: <0.00-1.00>
REASON: <one sentence explaining BOTH scores>

structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of disentangling similarity and relatedness in topic models, which are used to discover latent thematic structures in a corpus of documents. The authors introduce a novel two-dimensional evaluation framework that explicitly disentangles taxonomic similarity from thematic relatedness in topic models, addressing a fundamental gap in how the semantic structure of topics is evaluated. They construct a large-scale synthetic benchmark of word pairs with LLM-annotated annotations and train a neural scorer to quantify both semantic dimensions. The authors apply this scorer to a comprehensive evaluation of topic models across multiple corpora, revealing that different model families capture distinct semantic structures in their topics. They also demonstrate that similarity and relatedness scores successfully predict downstream task performance, depending on task requirements. The paper establishes similarity and relatedness as essential axes for topic model evaluation and provides a reliable pipeline for characterizing these across model families and corpora.

nodes.title: Disentangling Similarity and Relatedness in Topic Models
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the integration of pre-trained language models into topic models, which has led to a shift in how topics capture semantic structure. The authors highlight the difference between classical models, such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), and PLM-augmented models, which impose a prior that favors clustering of semantically similar words. The document introduces the concepts of thematic relatedness and taxonomic similarity, and proposes a method to disentangle these dimensions in topic models using a neural scoring function. The authors evaluate their approach across multiple corpora and topic model families, demonstrating that different models capture distinct semantic structure, and that similarity and relatedness scores can predict downstream task performance. The document establishes similarity and relatedness as essential axes for topic model evaluation and provides a pipeline for characterizing these across model families and corpora.

nodes.title: Abstract
